body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: PIM Join Attributes for Locator/ID Separation Protocol (LISP) Environments

[Laughter] Hey, >> take a picture of this. >> The recording is not going to show how many people we have unless you guys say something. So, just agree. So, wave back for the picture. Okay. So, um this is about RC860 BIS the LCAF uh work. So just a background just in case uh you missed this uh AD60 is an experimental RC that defines the cononical address format for list and as many as the other work that we're doing here we're moving it to a standard to the standard track. Um the idea is to not just update 8060 but 8060 is updated by 9306 and by the lisp geo draft that is going through the IG right now and both are experimental. So eventually we will wrap them into this one. Um there is also some work in 8378 biz that is in the status track that normatively depends on 8060. Right? So we want to take everything and move it to the sus track with as little changes as possible. Right? So the idea is to not change the technical part just to change the formatting and the structure and editorially and stuff like that. So this draft was already uh adopted by the working group. That's what 00 was. It was a copy of 8060 just with a new formatting and uh some of the references that were pointing to all drafts. Uh we are now at 02 and uh all that we've done is move things around. So we addressed the onea that was there which was like a one letter error that was in the original uh RFC. Um, I changed some of the uh references to point to the right places. And um, the main change is that I got rid of everything that talked about it being an experiment or what it originally called unapproved types. Uh, this was a very weird thing because they were not approved, but they were in the draft and they were not in the registry. And so they have type numbers but they were not um anywhere. And that's the main uh thing to talk about here which is what do we do with the unapproved types? Um because again they're not in the registry and the question is should we include them? Uh the answer is yes. If they're implemented we should include them because that means someone is using them. So there is a value assigned. They should be in the in the registry. Um what I really want to ask the working group here is what do we do with the ones that are not implemented and we have two options. One is we include them and we put them in the registry because you know they they are defined there could be implementations just because no one has reported something it doesn't mean that they don't exist. Uh or the other option is we could deprecate them. Now, before all of you run to the mic, uh I I I want to show the the six types that were the unapproved types before. And so we did get reports on the list from open daylight, Cisco, and the listers implementation about four of them that are implemented. Uh so those four I think it's a very easy decision. We need to include them. we need to promote them, I'm going to say to the standard and and keep them in the draft. There's the type six, which is the opaque key, and 8, which is a nonslocator that no one has reported an implementation of. Uh, again, that doesn't mean that they're not implemented. It just means that no one has reported anything. So, the real question is, what do you think we should do with those? Stig. >> Yeah. Hi, Stig here. Um, so how big is the space? The 256. >> It's 256. Yes. >> Yeah. Yeah. So I don't see any harm in putting them there. The space is big enough. I wonder a bit for the future u if the working group somehow should go away or something. How can people get future types and would it require like an RFC or could it be just a draft or >> Right. So everything right now I believe is um I forget what it is. Is it it uh uh I think it's uh RFC required or something along those lines. Uh and I'm looking at you Luji because I think you're the expert on that uh registry. Um the other thing is that the 255 is reserved for vendor specific. Uh so theoretically you can go and do a lot of other stuff there if you really wanted to uh on the vendor specific code point. Uh but yes, I mean to your point, we have what 16 17 with the new geo one uh out of 256 uh come you know so there's still I don't know almost 240 numbers left that that we could use so it shouldn't be a problem. Um now were you suggesting stick that we should add uh like we did for py that sort of extension thing that uh we could do subtypes later or something or I mean it's too early for that I think. >> Yeah it's too early for that. I'm just wondering how how strict it should be going forward. But um yeah >> as of today future assignment is specification required. >> Okay. Specification required. So it's not uh strict at all. Right. >> Okay. So you can just write a a draft say that's a specification and you can get >> you don't even need a draft for specification. >> It just needs to be somewhere. >> It just needs to be written down somewhere. >> Yeah. So that sounds good to me. and and yeah if if it starts running out in some years then you can always do subtypes or something. >> This is Dino. Um so for type eight I remember Job wanted it to do something inside the network. He wanted to be able to have packet signatures for streams. And I don't know um if he ever used it and it was um he wanted he was using Cisco boxes and I don't remember if Cisco implemented that. Um I think they did but I don't think anybody has fessed up to that. So I don't know what to do about it and we don't have an RFC that tells you how to do it exactly. So so that might be yet another case is that these may have values but no good value uh for for deployment but um there's no RFC's to say what they do. Um, so and same with opaque. I know opaque key was going to be used to to encode security fingerprints and opaque data where you don't want to label it as security keys because that kind of gives it away. So I don't think anybody implemented the opaque key. So >> right. So the other the other point about this is that if we deprecated them, we would probably still want to deprecate the value in the registry which would mean we lose the value, right? So it's it's probably a better choice to keep everything defined because that way if people are in fact using it even if they haven't told us then it doesn't matter. I mean we're already defined everyone knows how to use how to do it or how to at least encode it and uh we go from there. >> So great we have working group consensus at least in the room for this >> huge consensus >> unanimous consensus. Wow. I've never seen that anywhere else. >> Keep it small. >> Yes. So, uh, what are the next steps with this? Um, this should be, you know, relatively straightforward. Um, the idea is, you know, that numbering over there, uh, the numbers might change, but the idea is any reviews that came from from here and from the working group, we put into a three, then we do some maybe editorial changes. One of the things that we need to do um based on maybe the discussion earlier in in routing is put uh operational considerations into the draft uh and think about that. Um then have the work review merge the vendor specific stuff merge eventually leo uh the the geo list geo stuff and then be ready for working call. Uh most of this is incorporating things right that the working has already reviewed. Um we might want I think we do want to make sure that the descriptions are consistent across the whole draft that you know the names are consistent you know things like that. That's what I mean by editorial changes. Um but it shouldn't represent you know significant changes to to anything. uh with any luck we can just handle all of this on the list and and you know be ready I don't know sometime in the next few months uh finish the whole work so that's it >> sure >> I locked the queue because Okay, a very a very very quick update on on JAN should take two minutes even less maybe. So basically yeah we we sent a refresh um we add minor edits some acronyms and some acknowledgements. Um what I wanted to to show is this slide. This is the plan that we have for for the JAM model which is basically following the LCAF evolution. So we will incorporate the geo coordinates and the vendor laf to the junk model uh you know following the releases for the for the new 806. So basically we can uh coordinate Alvar and whenever you release anyone I'll I'll update the the Jack model and after we have the new new coordinates and the and the vendor in the in the model we can go to working group plus call and that's it so quick and easy right >> you are up next >> yes u I hope I'm audible good evening everyone I I hope it's even >> your Can you speak up a little bit because >> Yeah. >> Is it better now? >> Yeah. >> Okay. Okay. Good evening everyone. Um so I'm Prasad from Cisco. Um so this is >> the the control of the slides so you can >> Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. Thank you. So I will actually spend more time on the next one. uh uh but probably I'll just try to refresh uh what uh the the deployments draft has uh gone through uh so you know uh thanks to Dino we had some comments uh we had actually some good uh terminology corrections done uh not just terminology corrections I don't know maybe the right word uh probably I would say a reasonable amount of rewrite has been done to um to particularly uh link the uberlay uh draft, right? I think that is one of the more important pieces that I wanted to really cover in the uh in the deployments draft because that is where you know we we found the most complicated cases of multiccast to be because when you have you know more than one list fabric or another you know stitched fabrics whatever you want to call it the uberlay architecture right then essentially the the RTRs or the re-encapsulating uh routers really play a very crucial role and one of the important lessons that we had learned was to kind of sep separate out the multiccast ranges especially in the underlay that we use for the different uh you know list sites. Uh Dino go ahead want to say something we left room. Okay thank thank you. So so yeah and minor editorial things like signal free was excluded from the scope and and and one of the things that stick pointed out last time was to really add reference to references to pim light. This is particularly because the list pim over pim does not exchange hellos in the overlay and that is one of the reasons why we added the references to the pin light because the pim light already does that and we also clarified the coexistence of uniccast and multiccast underlays. Again this is something that has been also been used in deployment particularly with with different overlay address families and of course yeah added the the uberlay draft. Now the one question I have is now this draft now automatically adds a dependency to the uh list overlay draft and you know uh I I believe the list overlay uberlay draft I'm sorry the risk overlay draft will also have to be kind of adopted in in synchronization with the current draft because I don't know how that process works maybe the chairs or any of the others uh members can guide me on that uh on how to do whenever that that needs to be done. So, so this is the overlay uh you know kind of uh what do you call the overall reference diagram. uh the the key point here is essentially the RTRS that are that are at least in the Cisco terminology it's called border routers but whatever terminology we use essentially we terminate the list cap solution for example if if you have the source in site A and you know your receivers everywhere right you know it doesn't matter wherever they are then essentially the RTRS kind of play a very crucial role in try to terminate the outer list encapsulation add a new list encapsulation and that gives us the flexibility to do underlay v4 in one side, underlay v6 in another site uh or for that matter ingress replication on one side and and native multiccast on the other side. kind of gives us you know a whole lot of flexibility and I think the Uber app gives all the terminology that is needed to you know cover this use case and and uh thanks to the authors of the Uber app you know kind of for really thoughtfully laying it out because because initially when I started writing out the deployment draft I was a little bit you know uh I was unaware of the Uber draft first let me mention that so uh you know and then I was really wondering how to cover all the terminologies especially in in the IETF uh you uh what do you call ITF friendly way or ITF known way and the Uber app really provided that uh that framework and and so that was done uh you know so that that really in my understanding that really added a value to the uh to to this trap particularly to make the context clearer I mean the point I'm trying to make is the Uber wrap lays the foundation from which the deployment graph can really build upon it I think that's that's what I really wanted to try to say and then yeah we have added some new references also So uh some of them are ITF drafts for example the list VPN draft thanks Dino for pointing that out in the list group mapping draft they're all you know working group drafts and and and and you know there's no problem there but I think the only point you know is the overlay draft and I maybe you know I I probably need some guidance on how to you know really u kind of request adoption because I don't want to request adoption until I know how the overlay draft will be taken forward or not taken forward. So that's where I need some guidance. Yeah, that's mostly it from this side from this draft. Uh, you know, if there are any comments or questions, you can take it now or later also is fine. >> This is Dino. >> Uh, Prasad, are your intentions to want the uh Uber draft to be a working group document? >> Yes. Yes. >> Okay. In my understanding that that is a very useful work uh you know that kind of lays the base design for the uh you know uh the list architecture or whatever you know in the technology we still call it multiight but doesn't matter you know it that is lays the foundation so that's very important um as for the the adoption of this document I would say we we take it directly to the list Many people are here. We are not representative of the whole let's say ecosystem. So and we can think uh uh Victor about the Uber. So maybe also start. >> Perfect. Next slide. Yeah, thank you. Thank you. So I'm sorry I don't have very detailed slides but I think we can probably you know definitely discuss a lot. Uh this is you know uh again functionally no new work. You know essentially what I'm trying to do or rather what we are trying to do is take 8059 and 9798 both experimental and make them standard track. So we don't want to add any functionally new stuff. The only stuff that we want to add is probably editorial and organization wise. You know functionally it should be the same as whatever was implemented or rather you know whatever was specified as experimental. Now we can stand track. Now now why 8059 and why 9798 you're probably all aware but I'll just refresh the memory just just for historical purposes. AT59 lays the groundwork for the lisp specific two joint prune attributes. Uh you know uh why are they needed? They are primarily needed for two reasons. One is you know the first TLV uh that 8059 defines is a transport type TLV which is needed for the sides to signal you know which transport or which underlay transport is is are they using to receive the multiccast stream. So there are four possible combinations you know they could be using uniccast which is headend replication or multiccast and inside them v4 or v6. So you know any list site can send the same stream on multiple transports more than one uh because there could be arlocks that are preferring one you know one or the other right or even multiple you know is possible right and and and so 8059 kind of the the the transport type lays that that framework and and uh you know that's very useful I mean essentially that is the bedrock of the this uh multiccast implementation so without that we will not be able ble to really signal this preference. uh it would have to be a very homogeneous underlay which is not always possible especially with the overlay kind of architectures right so that is very very uh important and needed and and that TLB is exactly reused as what is defined in AT59 there is no there are no changes uh at least to what I know I mean if there are any changes please point out and we can definitely discuss if at all the changes are needed and if so why right and uh of course this is one of the last few documents that is undergoing the EXP to STD BIS process I mean this is probably added for the other working groups not not really is is a very obvious point just to state the obvious. uh the second TLV is what really was the uh you know uh that is why the second TLV is the reason why 9798 really came into existence and that is essentially the receiver ETR rlock TLB and that kind of says if you're using ingress replication which was 8059 right initial version and what is the receiver Rlock address that the the ETR wants to use so that the encapsulated stream can be sent to that ETRlock address you know in the case of the head and replication and and in case of native multiccast what what is the underlay multiccast that is to be used. Now this is again very very useful even with just a multiccast especially with the Uberlay we we we use different multiccast ranges you know uh uh on the either sides of the border I mean the RTR so essentially you do uh some kind of split horizon where you use different multiccast address ranges and this TLV gives us uh essentially what we need you know both for all the uh four cases you know what I talked about uh in the first TLV so just two simple TLVs uh you that really gets the job done for lisp underlay uh all four combinations uh different address families different uh transports uh and and that is essentially what the two documents had really specified and what I tried to do is essentially take 8059 and 9798 and kind of merge the text so uh it's not a straightforward or a simple merge you have to really re you know uh how how do I say it kind of uh in some sense rewrite the textual part of it but we don't want the meaning to get or rather change you know there is no intended functional change so by the process of translating it into a single document or merging it into a single document if I had done I mean if we had done any mistake or you know overlooked anything please let us know please review the draft in that sense it's very important review and uh you know at least at least the intention is to not make any functional changes just exactly the same uh content as 8059 and 9798 but in the form of a single document maybe the flow has to be slightly reorganized so that it it kind of reads nicely as one document. I know what was written in two documents. So that's that's the only thing that we wanted to do. So that that is the background of the document and uh yeah uh I I think most of the details are covered. The only point I wanted to discuss was I think uh you know stick already provided some guidance on this that the ANA code points we intend to reuse them. We don't want new code points to be defined or you know new new values to be allocated because obviously interrupt or backward compatibility is the main main reason why we want to reuse the same code point. So whatever language is needed to essentially say that whatever the experimental drafts the code points are located I mean experimental RFC I'm sorry the the the code points were located we will try to reuse them you know by whatever process the IDF needs to follow for that we'll try to do it [Music] so yeah that's mostly it from my side uh any comments or questions uh we are on the list you are welcome >> on the code point side you can certainly you reuse it's just in the Ayana section you ask to update the reference in the registry with with the new RFC number to come at some point that's all >> yes >> thank you thank you thank you Right. So, these are just some general comments about heads up for the working group. Um, I I'll do the easy one first and the one that's huge. Second, uh, we have that transport draft that decided to take Quick out. Um, I'm just wondering if we should do work on that. Number one. Number two, multiccast quick is becoming popular and it might provide some good scale for the mapping system. So, that could be another item of work. These things all fall under the current agenda, but these are just different mechanisms. So, just an FYI, should we take it on? Should somebody sign up for it? Should we just give up or whatever? You know, I don't know. >> So, that's the first part. You guys want to respond? >> Sorry. you know what is the multiccast quick stuff that you're referring to? >> Oh, I I thought we off moved off your presentation and just are doing general discussion. >> Yeah. Yeah, sure. Sure. >> Yeah. It's really I'm looking at uh Alberto and and Luigi for an answer or reaction. >> I think that Prasad is asking you what you mean by the multiccast quick. So, I think he didn't get what you were saying. >> Oh, okay. So what so I'll just say what is multiccast quick? So quick has been modified to be able to send data streams um quick connections could be opened up for multiccast data streams where all the data is sent on a multiccast address assuming that the underlay is running native multicast i.e pigg or whatever. Um it's a very interesting proposal because it's finally doing reliable multiccast and have taken lessons learned from decades of reliable multiccast and just very simply it sends multiccast and the loss detection and correction is done with FEC on the receiver. So retransmissions are really kept to a small minimum. when FDC can't repair the packet at the receiver, what it does is it asks uh acts are sent uniccast and retransmissions are done with uniccast. So it provides a reliable layer that we could depend on. So are there any applications or can lisp as an application use multiccast quick? Well, if we use like this like like what we have in the decentralized uh list draft where you can use multiccast to be able to send registers to a huge array of map servers, quick would be great. It would be great because it would multiccast it and it would be reliable all in the same time. So, so that's that's just a work item that we could look at if the working group decides >> the point would be uh who who will do the work. But anyway, I think the best way the best the best way is to open the discussion on the mailing list. I mean, >> okay, I'll do that. Uh no, we could look for val volunteers and you know of course I would help you know bring in a new generation of whispers maybe. Okay. So the second thing is from what I've been hearing this week is um a lot of people are talking about agent to agent with AI stuff and I see what's coming is interoperability between legacy devices and these software agents these virtual things and it appears that from I've been talking to a lot of people that these agents are going to probably be mobile and people are having problems keeping track of them and agents are going to pop up and go away like microservices. they're going to move around to be closer to where the maybe the models are being hosted um for low latency. Uh so I'm thinking that this is yet a a a new type of mobility play that does fall under the lisp charter and I think we should do something about that because there is seems to be a lot of interest and I I think if the Lisp working group doesn't do anything um that there'll be other solutions that won't be as as good as I I think Lisp could solve this problem. So you guys have any reaction to that? This is kind of like the future. This is quite huge. I think >> same thing. Take take it to the list. >> I mean I see your point. Um makes sense is interesting. Yes. But again who will do it? And uh I think the best way is again to to open the discussion on the mailing list who is interested and uh see if if we we have a a quorum of people that are willing to to do the work. >> Yeah. So I'm bringing just bringing it to your attention here physically, but I'll bring it to the list and then I'll make it two separate threads for the these two questions. >> Yeah. Better better. and then um and then we could try to solicit people because I think there's people that are outside the lisp ecosystem that might want to work on it. So I I think we could get some volunteers. I just wanted to make sure if it's okay if the working group wants to work on these things or you know we have to get a um send out a survey or whatever to see if they want to do it. >> Okay. Thanks Dino and unless there are other comments or that people who want to bring up I think we are done for today. >> Coffee time. >> Okay. Bye-bye. >> Sorry Padma. Go ahead. >> Thank you. Thank you. >> No, just wanted to say thank you. >> Okay. Byebye. Fadma, Prasad, everybody. >> Thank you. I'm paying this.
